era_context: Identical to previous four years: Old Kingdom Egypt (Dynasty VI) in decline, Akkad Empire under Naram-Sin, Indus Valley Civilization in mature phase, Xia Dynasty (China) either early or legendary. Bronze Age Eurasia.

events.title: Harappan civilization at peak population and urban development
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization reaches its maximum population and territorial extent during the mature Harappan phase. Major cities such as Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, Dholavira, and Kalibangan are fully developed and densely populated. The civilization covers an area of approximately 1.3 million square kilometers, making it larger than contemporary Egypt or Mesopotamia. This represents the height of Harappan demographic and economic power.

sources.name: Archaeological surveys and excavations of Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The mature Harappan phase is archaeologically well-established. -2044 falls within the period of maximum extent (c. 2600–1900 BCE).

events.title: Late Old Kingdom Egypt: administrative fragmentation accelerates
events.description: During Pepi II's reign (traditional chronology), the administrative fragmentation of Egypt continues to accelerate. Provincial governors (nomarchs) are accumulating wealth and power, and the central government's authority is weakening. This period marks the transition from the centralized Old Kingdom to the decentralized First Intermediate Period. Economic resources are increasingly concentrated in provincial hands rather than the royal treasury.

sources.name: Autobiographical inscriptions of provincial governors from this period

events.certainty_note: The general trend of administrative decentralization in late Dynasty VI is well-attested. Specific dating of -2044 within this process is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of a sudden 'collapse' of the Old Kingdom at the end of Dynasty VI is increasingly questioned by modern scholarship. Recent work suggests a more gradual transition and questions whether the administrative changes were as catastrophic as once believed. Additionally, the extent of Pepi II's actual reign length remains disputed.
historiographic_note: The year -2044 is archaeologically indistinguishable from surrounding years. No specific events are recorded for this individual year. The historical record for this period is so sparse that year-by-year differentiation is largely artificial. Information comes from broad archaeological patterns, king lists, and administrative records rather than year-specific documentation. The five-year span of -2040 to -2044 represents a single, largely undifferentiated period in the historical record.

graph_edges.note: The administrative fragmentation of late Dynasty VI led directly to the political fragmentation of the First Intermediate Period.
